title: Founding of Rome (traditional date)
summary: According to Roman tradition, on 21 April 753 BCE the twin Romulus founded a city on the Palatine Hill above the Tiber after killing his brother Remus in a quarrel over auguries. The date, fixed by the antiquarian Marcus Terentius Varro in the first century BCE, became the official birthday of Rome and the anchor of the Roman calendar (ab urbe condita). Archaeology tells a slower, less dramatic story of hilltop villages gradually coalescing during the eighth century BCE, but the myth would shape Roman identity for a thousand years.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
By the middle of the eighth century BCE, central Italy was a patchwork of small polities in flux. To the north the Etruscans were building the walled cities of Veii, Tarquinia and Caere; to the south, Greek colonists from Chalcis and Corinth were founding Cumae and Neapolis on the Bay of Naples. Between them stretched Latium, a fertile plain of scattered villages sharing a common Latin dialect and a common cult of Jupiter Latiaris on the Alban Mount. The seven hills above the marshy bend of the Tiber, where a ford and a salt route from the coast crossed, had been occupied since at least the tenth century BCE.

Into this landscape Roman tradition placed the story of Romulus and Remus, twin sons of the Vestal Rhea Silvia by the war god Mars. Their great-uncle Amulius, having usurped the throne of the Latin city of Alba Longa from his brother Numitor, ordered the infants exposed on the Tiber. They were suckled instead by a she-wolf in a cave at the foot of the Palatine called the Lupercal, later found and raised by the shepherd Faustulus and his wife Acca Larentia. On coming of age they killed Amulius, restored their grandfather, and set out to found a city of their own.

The tale reaches us through Livy's Ab urbe condita, the parallel Greek narrative of Dionysius of Halicarnassus, and Plutarch's Life of Romulus, all composed centuries after the fact and drawing on lost Roman annalists. The traditional date of 753 BCE itself was calculated by Marcus Terentius Varro in the 40s BCE, working backwards through king-lists and consul lists; his figure, adopted by the emperors Claudius and Hadrian for the city's ludi saeculares, became canonical. Ancient sources also offered 752, 751 and even 728 BCE, and the whole chronology has always been recognised as an approximation.

Behind the legend, excavation on the Palatine and in the Forum Boarium since the 1980s has revealed traces of Iron Age huts, defensive ditches and a rampart datable to somewhere between 775 and 725 BCE, roughly consistent with the traditional foundation window. The unification of the Palatine, Capitoline and Quirinal villages into a single community with civic buildings appears to have taken shape over the following century.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
In Livy's account the two brothers, unable to agree which of the seven hills should carry the new city or which of them should give it his name, resorted to augury. Remus, standing on the Aventine, saw six vultures first. Romulus, on the Palatine, then saw twelve. Each claimed victory, on precedence or on number, and their followers came to blows. Romulus killed Remus, or watched him killed, and traced the sacred boundary, the pomerium, around the Palatine with a bronze ploughshare drawn by a white bull and a white cow. When Remus, in the last version of the quarrel, leapt scornfully over the low new wall, Romulus struck him down with the shouted line, So perish anyone who leaps over my walls.

Romulus named the city Roma after himself, opened it as an asylum for fugitives from the surrounding Latin and Sabine communities in order to swell its population, and then, according to tradition, secured wives for his men by inviting the neighbouring Sabines to a festival and abducting their unmarried daughters, the famous Rape of the Sabine Women. The subsequent war with the Sabine king Titus Tatius ended in a merger of the two peoples on the Capitoline; Romulus and Tatius reigned jointly until Tatius was murdered at Lavinium.

Romulus is credited by later writers with the first Roman institutions: the Senate of one hundred patres, the division of the citizenry into three tribes and thirty curiae, the priestly college of the Salii, and the augural rites that framed every subsequent Roman public act. He was said to have ruled thirty-seven years and to have vanished, snatched up to heaven in a thunderstorm on the Campus Martius, thereafter worshipped as the god Quirinus.

How much, if any, of this reflects real events is unrecoverable. What archaeology can say is that between roughly 750 and 600 BCE a genuine, walled town did coalesce on the hills above the Tiber, that its early public spaces, including a paved Forum and the shrine known as the Lapis Niger, took shape in the seventh century, and that from the late seventh century a line of kings, some with Etruscan names such as Tarquinius, ruled the community until they were expelled in the traditional year 509 BCE. The Romulus story is a mythologised memory of that formative period, retold with the moral and political needs of much later generations in mind.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
Whatever really happened on the Palatine in the mid-eighth century BCE, the tradition itself became one of the most consequential foundation myths in history. The date 753 BCE was written into Roman public life. Years were counted ab urbe condita, and secular games in 249 BCE, 149 BCE and again under Augustus, Claudius, Domitian and Philip the Arab marked the city's anniversaries with theatrical splendour. In 248 CE the emperor Philip presided over the celebration of Rome's supposed millennium.

Under the kings and, from 509 BCE, the Republic, Rome grew from a Latin town of a few thousand into the dominant power of the Italian peninsula. The Twelve Tables were codified around 450 BCE, the Latin League was subordinated after 338 BCE, and by 275 BCE, after the defeat of King Pyrrhus of Epirus at Beneventum, Rome controlled all of peninsular Italy. The First Punic War against Carthage (264 to 241 BCE) then took her overseas for the first time, into Sicily and, by 146 BCE, into the whole western Mediterranean.

The legend of Romulus was reworked to fit each stage of that expansion. Augustus, who traced his own family through Aeneas of Troy to Venus and thus to Mars, restored the reputed Hut of Romulus on the Palatine and rebuilt the Temple of Quirinus. Virgil's Aeneid, commissioned in his reign, spliced the Trojan Aeneas story to the Romulus story to give Rome a pedigree at once Greek and native. The Ara Pacis, dedicated in 9 BCE, and the Forum of Augustus, with its gallery of statues of great Romans headed by Aeneas and Romulus, made the foundation myth into imperial iconography.

The physical Rome that grew up around the Palatine kept the legend visible. Visitors to the Forum could see the Lapis Niger, which some Romans believed marked Romulus's tomb; on the Capitoline they could see the great bronze she-wolf, later paired with the suckling twins added in the Renaissance, that still gives Rome its most instantly recognisable image.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The traditional founding of Rome shaped western political imagination for two and a half thousand years. The word republic (res publica) itself entered European vocabularies through Rome, and the ideal of a city with a fixed birthday, a founding hero, a set of ancestral laws and a mission to rule was consciously copied by dozens of later states, from the Italian communes of the twelfth century to revolutionary France and the United States. American founders debating the constitution in 1787 argued in the pages of The Federalist under the pseudonym Publius, drawn from the Roman Republic, and covered their public buildings in Roman columns and Latin mottoes.

Within Rome itself the foundation story remained alive as ritual and as landscape. The festival of the Parilia on 21 April, an ancient shepherds' rite for the goddess Pales, was reinterpreted from at least the late Republic onwards as the birthday of the city and is still marked by the modern Comune di Roma with parades in period costume. The Capitoline Wolf, whether Etruscan of the fifth century BCE or (as some current scholarship argues) a medieval casting, remains the emblem of the city and appears on lamp posts and manhole covers across Rome.

Modern historians are cautious about the details. The traditional date is understood as a Varronian construction rather than an eyewitness memory; the character of Romulus is a legendary composite; the twins may originate in an older Indo-European pattern of divine brothers. Yet the archaeological record does show a real settlement rising on the Palatine in the eighth century BCE, walled and organised, and it is that settlement, however it began, that grew into the city whose language, law and public architecture still frame much of the world.

worldContext.text: The mid-eighth century BCE was the era of the Neo-Assyrian resurgence. Tiglath-Pileser III, who took the throne in 745 BCE, reorganised the army, deported conquered populations across the empire, and pushed Assyrian power west into Syria and south into Babylonia. In Egypt the Twenty-Fifth Dynasty of Kushite kings from Napata in modern Sudan was consolidating its hold over the Nile valley; Piye's conquest of Memphis around 727 BCE was only a generation away. The Phoenician cities of Tyre and Sidon were at the height of their western trading networks.

In China the Western Zhou dynasty had begun its long decline. The move of the capital east to Luoyang in 771 BCE, forced by the sack of Haojing by Quanrong tribesmen, opened the Eastern Zhou period and the loose confederation of feudal states that would in time become the Spring and Autumn era. Regional lords such as those of Qi, Jin and Chu were beginning to eclipse the Zhou king. Bronze ritual vessels of exceptional craftsmanship continued to be cast for aristocratic ancestor cults.

The later Vedic period was underway on the Ganges plain. The great sacrificial rituals described in the Shatapatha Brahmana and the earliest Upanishads were being codified, and the Sanskrit-speaking Kuru and Panchala kingdoms of the upper Doab dominated the region politically. Iron working, first appearing in the Painted Grey Ware culture of the previous century, was spreading, enabling forest clearance and the beginnings of urban settlement that would flower in the sixth century BCE as the sixteen mahajanapadas.

In the Andes the Chavín cult centred on the highland shrine of Chavín de Huántar was reaching its mature phase, its stone reliefs of jaguars, caymans and fanged deities spreading across coastal and highland Peru. In Mesoamerica the Olmec civilisation of the Gulf Coast, its colossal basalt heads carved at San Lorenzo and La Venta, was in its late florescence. Both cultures would provide iconographic vocabularies that later Andean and Mesoamerican civilisations continued to draw on.

South of Egypt the Kushite kingdom of Napata was rising to imperial power. Its king Kashta had extended influence into Upper Egypt by around 760 BCE, and his successor Piye would within a generation conquer the whole Nile valley to the Delta. Along the North African coast, Phoenician colonists had already founded Utica and were about to found Carthage; tradition places its foundation by Dido of Tyre in 814 BCE, giving Rome and Carthage almost the same legendary birth century.

The Lapita cultural complex, which around 1500 to 1000 BCE had spread from the Bismarck Archipelago through Vanuatu and New Caledonia to Fiji, Tonga and Samoa, was transitioning into distinct regional traditions. In western Polynesia, ancestors of later Polynesian societies were consolidating in Tonga and Samoa, developing the maritime and horticultural skills that would eventually carry their descendants out to eastern Polynesia and, much later, to Hawaii and Aotearoa New Zealand.

figures.name: Romulus
figures.role: Legendary founder and first king of Rome, traditionally reigned 753 to 716 BCE before his apotheosis as the god Quirinus

figures.name: Remus
figures.role: Twin brother of Romulus, killed in the dispute over the foundation of the city

figures.name: Marcus Terentius Varro
figures.role: First-century BCE Roman antiquarian whose calculation fixed the traditional foundation date at 753 BCE

figures.name: Titus Livius (Livy)
figures.role: Augustan-era historian whose Ab urbe condita gave the fullest surviving Latin narrative of Rome's foundation and early kings

images.caption: The Capitoline Wolf in the Musei Capitolini, Rome. The bronze she-wolf, long dated to the fifth century BCE and now sometimes argued to be a medieval casting, was paired with the suckling twins Romulus and Remus in the fifteenth century.
images.credit: Photograph by Jastrow, 2006, via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: A 1904 print of the Capitoline Wolf with the twins, from the Bibliothek des allgemeinen und praktischen Wissens.
images.credit: Bibliothek des allgemeinen und praktischen Wissens (1904), via Wikimedia Commons
